ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Serunding Daging; closest match is the general meat floss food product class. Serunding Daging is a spiced, dry-fried shredded beef product traditional to Malay cuisine.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat-based composite dish — processed/dried meat product
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Serunding Daging. Code A0CKP for processed/dried meat composite is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Serunding Daging or equivalent Malay meat floss product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Serunding Daging is approximately 30 g (about 2 tablespoons), commonly used as a condiment alongside lemang, ketupat, or steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritional Composition of Malaysian Foods (IMR Malaysia MyFCD) — Beef-based processed meat products; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef, dried/spiced meat product (NDB 13352) for micronutrient anchoring
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Serunding Daging as a finished product. Macronutrient values are anchored to IMR Malaysia MyFCD entries for dried/spiced beef products and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef jerky and dried spiced beef entries. Micronutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef (cooked, lean) adjusted for concentration due to moisture loss during dry-frying. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 40% of total fat based on coconut-milk and beef fat composition typical of this preparation. Values are consistent with the input enriched_json record (confidence 0.9) and the cited JTAFS publication on Malaysian meat floss nutritional quality.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI based on analogous high-protein, high-fat dried meat product; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Serunding Daging in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy with high-protein, high-fat dried spiced meat products. The very high protein (28.5 g/100 g) and fat (28.2 g/100 g) content with low available carbohydrate (12.5 g/100 g, of which 3.5 g is fibre) strongly attenuates postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (35 × 9.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.15 / 100 × 30 ≈ 1.3 g. Estimation is consistent with the input record GI confidence of 0.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (550 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from coconut products
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Serunding Daging to small portions (≤30 g) due to its high sodium content of 550 mg per 100 g, which contributes approximately 165 mg sodium per standard serving, and its significant saturated fat load from kerisik and coconut milk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35), high protein and fat content attenuating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Serunding Daging has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of about 1.3 per 30 g serving, making it a suitable accompaniment for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions; however, added sugar in commercial preparations should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from coconut milk and kerisik, and high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (estimated ~11.3 g/100 g) derived from coconut milk and toasted coconut paste, combined with high sodium (550 mg/100 g), warrants moderation for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; portion size should be restricted to 30 g or less per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, galangal) present in concentrated form
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Serunding Daging contains concentrated anti-inflammatory spices including turmeric, ginger, and galangal, which may offer modest benefit for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, though high-heat dry-frying may reduce some bioactive potency.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Serunding Daging is a traditional Malay beef floss, a dry, intensely flavoured processed meat condiment made by slow-cooking beef with coconut milk, kerisik (toasted coconut paste), and a complex spice blend including lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, ginger, chili, and coriander, then dry-frying the shredded meat in a large wok over low heat for several hours until it becomes airy, fibrous, and shelf-stable. The result is a concentrated, spicy, savoury, and subtly sweet product with a distinctive golden-brown colour and a crumbly, floss-like texture. It is a festive staple in Malaysian Malay households, especially during Eid al-Fitr (Hari Raya Aidilfitri), and is traditionally served alongside lemang (glutinous rice cooked in bamboo), ketupat (compressed rice cake), or plain steamed rice. Nutritionally, Serunding Daging is energy-dense at approximately 420 kcal per 100 g, with high protein (28.5 g), significant fat (28.2 g, including saturated fat from coconut products), and moderate carbohydrate (12.5 g). Its sodium content is high at 550 mg/100 g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low estimated glycemic index (~35) and high protein-fat ratio make it suitable in small portions for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. It is a good source of iron (4.5 mg), zinc (5.2 mg), vitamin B12 (3.5 mcg), niacin (6.5 mg), and phosphorus (280 mg). The standard serving size is 30 g, used as a condiment rather than a main dish.

condiment served with lemang and ketupat

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Serunding Daging as a finished product in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central. Macronutrient values are adapted from IMR MyFCD dried/spiced beef product entries and cross-validated with USDA SR Legacy beef jerky data. Micronutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked lean beef adjusted for moisture concentration. Saturated fat is estimated at ~40% of total fat based on coconut-milk and beef fat composition. GI is an analogous estimate. The input enriched_json record carried confidence 0.9 from a pipeline source citing the JTAFS publication on Malaysian meat floss, which provides supporting nutritional context. Overall confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Serunding Daging as a finished spiced dry-fried product are not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 11.3 g/100 g (40% of total fat 28.2 g) based on the known fatty acid profile of coconut milk and beef fat typical of this preparation. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework for high-protein, high-fat dried meat products; no direct GI measurement for Serunding Daging was found. Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as (35 × 9.0 g available carbohydrate per 100 g × 0.30) / 100 ≈ 1.3. All core macros (protein_g 28.5, fat_g 28.2, carbohydrate_g 12.5) are non-zero and consistent with the high-protein, high-fat, moderate-carbohydrate profile of a concentrated spiced dried beef product. No zero-macro justification required. This is a Malay cuisine dish; multilingual requirements apply to Malay language only (not Singaporean dish rules). Malay local name and aliases are fully populated. The dish is halal by traditional preparation using beef and plant-based spices with no pork or alcohol.
